name: Video Game 'Feel' Taxonomy (Swink Game Feel; MDA Aesthetics; Lazzaro Four Keys; PENS)
core_claim: Games research's distinctive contribution is the insistence that what people enjoy is not reducible to what a work is ABOUT (its content, story, or setting) but is substantially constituted by what the moment-to-moment experience FEELS LIKE — and that this experiential dimension can be named, decomposed, and designed for independently of content. This 'feel-vs-content split' is the through-line across several overlapping frameworks the games field developed because, unlike film/TV criticism, it could not lean on plot summary: an interactive system has thin narrative content but rich experiential texture, forcing the field to build a vocabulary for experience itself. Four pillars: (1) Steve Swink's GAME FEEL ('Game Feel: A Game Designer's Guide to Virtual Sensation', 2009) defines feel as 'real-time control of virtual objects in a simulated space, with interactions emphasised by polish', and argues great feel delivers five experiences — the aesthetic sensation of control, the pleasure of skill mastery, extension of the senses (proprioception), extension of identity, and interaction with a unique physical reality — decomposed into a taxonomy of INPUT, RESPONSE, and CONTEXT plus a 'polish' layer (sound, juice, metaphor). (2) Hunicke, LeBlanc & Zubek's MDA framework (2004) splits a game into Mechanics -> Dynamics -> AESTHETICS, and crucially lists EIGHT KINDS OF FUN (Sensation, Fantasy, Narrative, Challenge, Fellowship, Discovery, Expression, Submission) as the emotional/experiential targets — explicitly arguing designers should stop saying 'fun' and name the specific aesthetic. (3) Nicole Lazzaro's FOUR KEYS TO FUN (2004), derived from facial-expression and observational research, names four affective routes to engagement — Hard Fun (challenge/fiero, the emotion of triumph over adversity), Easy Fun (novelty/curiosity/immersion), Serious Fun (changing how the player feels/thinks — relaxation, excitement, meaning), and People Fun (social emotions: amusement, schadenfreude, naches) — and finds best-selling games offer at least three. (4) PENS / Self-Determination-Theory applied to play (Ryan, Rigby & Przybylski, 2006) grounds enjoyment in satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs plus immersion/presence. The unifying claim: enjoyment is an emotional/need-satisfaction profile of the EXPERIENCE, orthogonal to subject matter; two games (or two films) about completely different things can deliver the same feel, and one subject can be wrapped in radically different feels.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Emphatically EXPERIENTIAL/AFFECTIVE, and self-consciously so — this body of work is arguably the clearest articulation anywhere of the descriptive-vs-experiential distinction, because the games field hit the limits of content-description earlier and harder than film criticism did. Each pillar occupies a slightly different slice of the experiential plane: Swink's Game Feel is the most embodied and lowest-level — the kinaesthetic, proprioceptive, tactile sensation of control and responsiveness (a 'feel' axis with almost no content at all); MDA's eight aesthetics are higher-level EMOTIONAL TARGETS (what the play is FOR, affectively — sensation, challenge, fellowship, etc.); Lazzaro's Four Keys are explicitly EMOTION categories (the framework is built on measured facial affect — fiero, curiosity, relaxation, amusement); PENS is MOTIVATIONAL/need-based (autonomy/competence/relatedness as the psychological substrate of enjoyment). None of them describe what the work is topically about. Note the medium gap: much of Swink's feel layer (proprioceptive control) has no analogue in non-interactive TV/film and does NOT transfer; the parts that transfer to a TV/anime recommender are the higher-level aesthetic/emotion/motivation layers (MDA aesthetics, Lazzaro's keys, PENS needs), which are genuinely medium-agnostic experiential dimensions.
key_dimensions: Pillar 1 — Swink Game Feel taxonomy: INPUT (how the player expresses intent), RESPONSE (how the system processes and reacts in real time — responsiveness, sensitivity, latency), CONTEXT (constraints/space giving the motion meaning), plus POLISH/'juice' (sound, particle/visual feedback, screen-shake, ancillary indicators), METAPHOR (the fiction wrapping the simulation), and the five target sensations: control, skill mastery, sensory extension, identity extension, unique physical reality. Mostly interactive-specific; largely non-transferable to passive viewing. Pillar 2 — MDA eight aesthetics (the most transferable list): SENSATION (sense-pleasure / spectacle), FANTASY (make-believe, escapist world), NARRATIVE (drama, story-driven), CHALLENGE (mastery/obstacle), FELLOWSHIP (social/community/co-experience), DISCOVERY (exploration, uncharted territory), EXPRESSION (self-discovery, creativity), SUBMISSION (game-as-pastime, comfort/ambient unwinding). Pillar 3 — Lazzaro Four Keys (emotion routes): HARD FUN (fiero/challenge/frustration-then-triumph), EASY FUN (curiosity/wonder/novelty/immersion), SERIOUS FUN (mood regulation / meaning / excitement-relaxation), PEOPLE FUN (social emotions — amusement, schadenfreude, naches, generosity). Pillar 4 — PENS/SDT: AUTONOMY (volition/choice), COMPETENCE (mastery/effectance), RELATEDNESS (social connection), plus PRESENCE/IMMERSION and INTUITIVE CONTROLS as enjoyment predictors. These are the candidate experiential axes for the vocabulary.
cross_cultural_validity: Mixed and unevenly tested. MDA and Swink's Game Feel are practitioner-derived design frameworks with essentially NO formal cross-cultural validation — they are conceptual taxonomies refined in (predominantly North American / Western) GDC workshops, so their universality is asserted, not demonstrated. Lazzaro's Four Keys rests on facial-expression coding, which leans on Ekman-style basic-emotion assumptions that are themselves cross-culturally contested; the named emotions (fiero, naches, schadenfreude) are deliberately borrowed from multiple languages, gesturing at cross-cultural reach, but the empirical sample is not systematically cross-cultural. The strongest cross-cultural evidence sits under PENS/Self-Determination Theory: SDT's three basic needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness) have been tested across many cultures and the PENS scale itself has been translated and validated (e.g. a validated Malaysian/MOBA-player adaptation), giving the motivational substrate the best universality claim of the four. What tends to break down cross-culturally: the relative weighting and acceptability of People Fun / Fellowship (collectivist vs individualist play norms), the salience of Expression/autonomy, and the specific affect labels. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Heterogeneous, ranging from practitioner-anecdotal to genuinely empirical. WEAKEST: MDA (a conceptual workshop framework, validated by design utility and citation rather than against enjoyment/completion data) and Swink's Game Feel (close-reading of games, designer introspection, case studies — persuasive but not experimental). MIDDLE: Lazzaro's Four Keys is grounded in original observational/physiological research (facial-expression and behavioural coding of players, the first to use facial affect to measure player emotion) but the studies are small, proprietary (XEODesign), and not published as peer-reviewed validation; it is better evidenced than MDA but well short of psychometric rigour. STRONGEST: PENS, which has peer-reviewed validation studies (e.g. Johnson, Gardner & colleagues validating PENS and GEQ; VR-exercise and MOBA validations) showing need satisfaction predicts enjoyment, intrinsic motivation, and sustained engagement — correlational/survey-based, occasionally experimental, anchored in the wider SDT evidence base. Evidence type overall: predominantly correlational and observational, with the design taxonomies being interpretive. To validate LLM-extracted dimensions against this framework you would need a corpus tagged for these experiential axes (e.g. challenge/discovery/fellowship/sensation profiles) with matched per-viewer enjoyment or completion outcomes, then test whether synopsis-derived experiential proxies predict outcomes better than content tags alone — and for the relational keys (People Fun, relatedness) you would need pair-level data, not just individual ratings.
operationalisability: Partially extractable, with a sharp split. The Swink game-feel layer is essentially NOT extractable for a TV/anime recommender — it describes real-time control sensation that does not exist in passive media; ignore it for HelpME2C except as a conceptual reminder to separate feel from content. The MDA aesthetics, Lazzaro keys, and PENS needs are MORE extractable because some map onto synopsis-visible signals: SENSATION/spectacle (a synopsis or marketing copy emitting 'visually stunning', action set-pieces); FANTASY/escapism (genre + setting cues); NARRATIVE-drivenness (plot-dense vs character/mood-driven); CHALLENGE has only a weak passive-media analogue (the tension/stakes/puzzle-to-follow a mystery offers the viewer); DISCOVERY (exploratory worldbuilding, mystery-box, unfamiliar settings); SUBMISSION/EASY-comfort (cosy, low-stakes, ambient, comfort-watch signals); SERIOUS FUN / mood regulation (uplifting, cathartic, relaxing, tear-jerker). Extraction unit: best as a WHOLE-WORK experiential profile (a distribution over aesthetics/keys), with arc-level estimation for serialised TV where tone shifts. Failure modes: (1) collapsing into content — an LLM will read 'fantasy setting' and tag FANTASY-aesthetic even when the FEEL is grim challenge, conflating subject with experience; (2) marketing copy asserting 'thrilling/heart-warming' regardless of the actual experience; (3) the relational keys (People Fun, Fellowship, relatedness) are NOT properties of the work and cannot be read off a synopsis as an enjoyment value — they describe a viewing CONTEXT (co-viewing) the work merely affords; (4) Hard Fun/Challenge has the weakest passive analogue and is the most likely to be force-fit; (5) Expression has essentially no passive-viewing analogue and should not be extracted.
motivation_served: This is the framework's centre of gravity: it is explicitly a theory of viewer/player MOTIVATION, and it spans the full hedonic-eudaimonic-mood-social range better than most single frameworks. Mapping: HEDONIC enjoyment -> Sensation/Easy Fun/spectacle and the pleasure of mastery (Hard Fun, competence); EUDAIMONIC appreciation/meaning -> Serious Fun ('changing how I feel/think', meaning) and Narrative aesthetic; MOOD REGULATION -> Serious Fun and Submission/comfort (the unwind/ambient pole); IDENTITY EXPRESSION -> Expression aesthetic and Fantasy (identity extension in Swink); SOCIAL CONNECTION -> People Fun, Fellowship aesthetic, and PENS relatedness. For GROUP viewing this body of work is unusually well-suited: Lazzaro's PEOPLE FUN and MDA's FELLOWSHIP are first-class categories built around shared/social experience, and PENS relatedness is a measured need — so the frameworks already contain the vocabulary for 'the joint experience two people have together', not just individual taste. The natural HelpME2C application: model each viewer's motivational profile across these axes (one wants Hard-Fun-analogue tension, the other Submission/comfort) and treat a good joint pick as one whose experiential profile satisfies a shareable subset — and recognise that the social keys can be SERVED BY the act of co-viewing itself, partially decoupling them from the work.
receiver_dependence: Strongly mixed, and the framework is comparatively explicit about it — more so than content taxonomies. WORK-SIDE (relatively absolute, synopsis-estimable): SENSATION/spectacle level, NARRATIVE-drivenness, the comfort/SUBMISSION vs intensity register, and DISCOVERY/exploratory worldbuilding are largely affordances the work offers regardless of viewer. RELATIONAL (emerge from work x viewer): CHALLENGE/Hard Fun depends on the viewer's skill, prior exposure, and tolerance for difficulty/ambiguity (a twisty plot is delicious challenge to one viewer, tiring to another); SERIOUS FUN / mood regulation is intrinsically state-and-need dependent (whether a film 'relaxes' or 'moves' you depends on your current mood and what you bring); EXPRESSION and identity-extension depend on whether the viewer's self resonates. PURELY CONTEXTUAL (not a work property at all): PEOPLE FUN / FELLOWSHIP / RELATEDNESS are properties of the viewing situation (who you watch with), only AFFORDED by the work. For a two-person recommender this trichotomy is the key takeaway: store SENSATION/comfort-register/narrative-drivenness/discovery as catalogue tags; model challenge-fit and mood-fit as relational user x work layers; and treat the social keys as a co-viewing-context layer that the recommender can actively leverage (the act of watching together can itself supply People Fun) rather than a value to read off the work.
blind_spots: (1) Medium-transfer failure: the most distinctive and best-developed pillar (Swink's proprioceptive game feel) simply does not exist in passive viewing, so the part of this literature that is most rigorous about 'feel' is the least usable for TV/anime. (2) Content-agnostic by design: these frameworks deliberately say nothing about WHICH subject matter a person wants — they describe the experiential envelope, not the theme, so they cannot drive topical recommendation alone (they are the complement to, not a replacement for, the theme vocabulary). (3) Fuzzy boundaries / non-orthogonality: the eight aesthetics and four keys overlap heavily (Sensation vs Easy Fun, Challenge vs Hard Fun, Narrative vs Serious Fun) and are not mutually exclusive or measured against each other, so they resist clean encoding. (4) Weak empirical floor for MDA and Game Feel (practitioner taxonomies, no enjoyment-data validation). (5) Affect-labelling assumptions in Lazzaro (basic-emotion / facial-coding premises that are cross-culturally contested). (6) Under-theorises pure NARRATIVE craft — 'Narrative' is one of eight boxes, so the framework is thin precisely where TV/film richness lives. (7) Largely silent on the difference between first-watch discovery and comfort RE-watching as distinct experiential modes (Submission gestures at it but doesn't develop it). (8) Built around active, often solo or competitive play; weak on the contemplative, slow, ambient co-presence common to TV co-viewing.
vocabulary_implications: This framework's main gift to HelpME2C is not new theme slugs but a cross-cutting EXPERIENTIAL/AFFECTIVE FACET layer that sits orthogonal to the topical theme vocabulary — exactly the 'feel vs content' split the field solved. Recommended: introduce a small controlled set of experiential-register tags, LLM-estimable from synopsis + marketing/critical signals, derived from the transferable axes: 'spectacle-sensation' (audiovisual sense-pleasure, set-pieces — MDA Sensation), 'comfort-watch' / 'low-stakes-ambient' (Submission + Easy Fun comfort pole; a mood-regulation/unwind register), 'cathartic' / 'mood-lift' (Serious Fun mood-regulation), 'wonder-discovery' (exploratory worldbuilding / mystery-box — MDA Discovery; partly overlaps existing 'first-contact', 'immersive world'), 'tension-and-mastery' (the passive analogue of Hard Fun/Challenge — puzzle/stakes the viewer actively tracks; overlaps 'mystery-investigation', 'whodunit'), and 'co-viewing-friendly' as a context flag rather than a work value. Encode the register tags as a separate facet, NOT mixed into the theme list. Flag explicitly as NOT extractable from synopsis / requiring other data sources or a relational layer: the SOCIAL keys (People Fun / Fellowship / relatedness — these belong in a co-viewing-context model, populated by who-is-watching, not catalogue text); CHALLENGE-FIT and MOOD-FIT (relational user x work, need per-user difficulty tolerance and current mood/state); EXPRESSION and proprioceptive game-feel (drop — no passive-viewing analogue). Also recommend an explicit 'experiential-register' axis (intense <-> comfort) as a single ordinal that complements rather than duplicates theme slugs.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Operates one level up from / orthogonal to the existing topical, moral, and identity slugs, and overlaps mainly with the TEXTURE and GENRE/STRUCTURE groups. It VALIDATES texture/structure slugs that are really experiential-register descriptors rather than topics: 'slow-burn' (an experiential pacing/register, a Submission/Serious-Fun dwell), 'dark-comedy' / 'absurdist-comedy' (People-Fun amusement register), 'character-study' and 'meditation-on-time'/'meditation-on-death' (Serious Fun / contemplative register), and the mystery cluster — 'mystery-investigation', 'whodunit', 'heist-and-caper', 'chase-and-escape' — which are as much CHALLENGE/tension EXPERIENCES (the viewer actively tracking and anticipating) as they are plot structures. It suggests these are MISALIGNED when filed alongside topical themes: like cognitive-narratology and transportation theory reached, the games-feel work argues pacing/register/challenge descriptors belong in a distinct experiential facet, not the theme list. GAPS it reveals: HelpME2C currently has (a) no explicit COMFORT/AMBIENT vs INTENSE register axis (the Submission/Easy-Fun comfort pole is unrepresented — there is no 'comfort-watch' or 'low-stakes' handle, yet this is a primary co-viewing decision variable); (b) no SPECTACLE/SENSATION tag distinct from genre; (c) no DISCOVERY/wonder register distinct from the speculative content slugs; (d) crucially, no SOCIAL/CO-VIEWING layer at all, despite People Fun / Fellowship / relatedness being first-class in this literature and despite HelpME2C's entire North Star being two-person joint viewing — this is the framework's most pointed contribution: the vocabulary lacks any representation of the SHARED experiential affordance, which is exactly the metric the product optimises.
key_sources: Swink, S. (2009). 'Game Feel: A Game Designer's Guide to Virtual Sensation', Morgan Kaufmann.
